questions.question: What service lifetime does `AddDbContext<AppDbContext>()` register `DbContext` with, and which one must you avoid?
options: Scoped by default; never register it as Singleton because `DbContext` is not thread-safe
Transient by default; never register it as Scoped because the change tracker would leak
Singleton by default; never register it as Transient because connections would not be pooled
Scoped by default; never register it as Transient because migrations would re-run
questions.explanation: `AddDbContext` registers the context as Scoped (one per request). It must never be a Singleton because `DbContext` is not thread-safe, so sharing one instance across concurrent requests corrupts the change tracker.

questions.question: What is the primary effect of calling `AsNoTracking()` on a query?
options: EF Core returns the results without putting them in the change tracker, so edits to those entities are not persisted by `SaveChanges`
EF Core opens a read-only database transaction so the rows cannot be modified by other sessions
EF Core caches the results in memory so subsequent identical queries skip the database
EF Core still tracks the entities but defers writing changes until the next `SaveChanges` call
questions.explanation: `AsNoTracking()` runs the query without adding results to the change tracker (less memory, faster reads). Because the entities aren't tracked, modifying them and calling `SaveChanges` is a no-op unless you reattach them.

questions.question: An entity in the **Unchanged** state generates what SQL when `SaveChanges` runs?
options: Nothing — its current values match its original values
An `UPDATE` touching every column, to be safe
A `SELECT` to re-validate the row still exists
An `UPDATE` touching only the primary key column
questions.explanation: Unchanged means the entity is tracked but identical to its original values, so `SaveChanges` emits nothing. Only Added (INSERT), Modified (UPDATE), and Deleted (DELETE) produce SQL.

questions.question: Which loading strategy most commonly causes the N+1 query problem?
options: Lazy loading — accessing a navigation in a loop fires one query per item
Eager loading — `Include` always issues a separate query per related entity
No-tracking queries — they re-query each row to resolve identity
Split queries — `AsSplitQuery` issues one query per row
questions.explanation: Lazy loading triggers a separate query each time a navigation is accessed, so iterating a list of N items produces 1 + N queries. Eager loading with `Include` is the fix, not the cause.

questions.question: Which exception does EF Core throw when an optimistic-concurrency token check matches 0 rows at save time?
options: `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`
`DbUpdateException`
`InvalidOperationException`
`OptimisticLockException`
questions.explanation: When the `UPDATE ... WHERE Id = @id AND RowVersion = @original` matches 0 rows (the version moved), EF throws `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`. `DbUpdateException` is its base type used for other update failures, and `OptimisticLockException` is not an EF Core type.

questions.question: You query by a column that *should* be unique (e.g. email). Two rows unexpectedly match. Which operator surfaces this data-corruption bug by throwing, rather than silently returning one row?
options: `SingleOrDefault(...)`
`FirstOrDefault(...)`
`First(...)`
`Where(...).Take(1)`
questions.explanation: `SingleOrDefault` returns null on no match but throws on more than one — the throw is a feature that surfaces a uniqueness violation. `FirstOrDefault` and `First` both happily return the first of several matches, hiding the corruption.

questions.question: You `Include` two **collection** navigations (`o.Items` and `o.Payments`) on the same query and notice a huge row blow-up. What does `AsSplitQuery()` do to address it?
options: Emits one SQL statement per collection navigation instead of one big JOIN, avoiding the cartesian product
Replaces the JOINs with a single subquery that deduplicates rows at the database
Loads only the first collection eagerly and lazy-loads the rest
Caps the number of related rows returned per parent to prevent the explosion
questions.explanation: Including multiple collection navigations in one JOIN multiplies rows (Items x Payments per order). `AsSplitQuery()` splits the work into one SQL statement per collection, avoiding the cartesian product.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code?
questions.code:
var users = await context.Users.ToListAsync();
var activeUsers = users.Where(x => x.IsActive);
options: `ToListAsync()` materializes **all** users into memory, then filters client-side; the `Where` should come before `ToListAsync` so it runs as SQL
`Where` returns an `IQueryable`, so `activeUsers` is never executed and the filter is lost
Mixing async (`ToListAsync`) with a synchronous `Where` causes a deadlock
`IsActive` cannot be translated to SQL, so EF throws at runtime
questions.explanation: Calling `ToListAsync()` first pulls every row into memory; the subsequent `Where` runs in app memory (LINQ to Objects), not the database. Putting `Where` before `ToListAsync` pushes the filter into SQL so only matching rows return.

questions.question: Why does the database remain unchanged after this code runs?
options: The entity was loaded with `AsNoTracking`, so it isn't in the change tracker and `SaveChanges` sees nothing to write
`FirstAsync` returns a detached copy by value, so the assignment mutates a temporary
`SaveChangesAsync` was not awaited inside a transaction, so the write was rolled back
`AsNoTracking` makes the entity read-only, so the `Name` assignment silently throws
questions.explanation: `AsNoTracking` keeps the entity out of the change tracker, so EF has no recorded modification to flush — `SaveChanges` is a no-op. Dropping `AsNoTracking` (or calling `context.Update(user)`) makes the edit persist.

questions.question: For a production app running **multiple instances**, which migration approach does the reviewer recommend, and why?
options: Generate an idempotent SQL script (`dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent`) and apply it through the deployment pipeline, because multiple instances racing `Database.Migrate()` on boot can conflict
Call `Database.Migrate()` at startup in every instance, because EF serializes concurrent migrations automatically
Call `EnsureCreated()` at startup, because it is safe to combine with migrations at scale
Apply each migration's `Up` method manually in code on every request to stay current
questions.explanation: For production the reviewer recommends an idempotent script applied via the pipeline; `Database.Migrate()` at startup is reserved for dev/single-instance because multiple instances racing it on boot can conflict. `EnsureCreated()` must not be mixed with migrations.

questions.question: Inside a `catch (DbUpdateConcurrencyException)` handler, what does `entry.CurrentValues.SetValues(databaseValues)` implement as a conflict-resolution strategy?
options: "Store wins" — it discards the user's edits and reloads the current database values
"Client wins" — it keeps the user's edits and overwrites the database unconditionally
A merge that combines the user's edits with the database values column by column
A rollback that re-throws the exception after restoring original values
questions.explanation: Setting `CurrentValues` to the database values discards the client's edits — "store wins". "Client wins" instead keeps `CurrentValues` and only re-baselines the token via `entry.OriginalValues.SetValues(databaseValues)`.

questions.question: Why does projecting to a DTO with `Select(x => new UserDto { ... })` implicitly avoid change-tracking overhead even without `AsNoTracking()`?
options: Because you aren't materializing tracked entity instances — the result is a DTO, which EF has no entity state to track
Because `Select` forces the query to run client-side, where tracking does not apply
Because EF automatically calls `AsNoTracking` whenever a `Where` clause is present
Because DTOs are immutable, so EF skips snapshotting them
questions.explanation: A projection produces DTO objects rather than entity instances, so there is nothing for the change tracker to snapshot — tracking is implicitly skipped. It also sends less data over the wire by selecting only needed columns.

questions.question: Which statements about EF Core lazy loading are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Lazy loading is off by default and requires the proxies package plus `virtual` navigation properties
With lazy loading disabled and no `Include`, an un-loaded **reference** navigation is `null`
With lazy loading disabled and no `Include`, an un-loaded **collection** navigation is an empty collection
Lazy loading is enabled by default and must be turned off to use `Include`
Lazy loading guarantees a single JOIN per parent entity
questions.explanation: Lazy loading is off by default and needs `UseLazyLoadingProxies()` plus `virtual` navs. When it's off and you don't `Include`, an un-loaded reference nav is null and a collection nav is empty. It is not on by default, and it produces extra per-access queries, not a single JOIN.

questions.question: Which of these are recommended EF Core query-performance techniques from the senior checklist? (Select all that apply.)
options: Project to a DTO with `Select` to fetch only needed columns
Use `AsNoTracking` for read-only queries
Inspect generated SQL with `query.ToQueryString()` before optimizing
Default every navigation to lazy loading so data loads only when touched
Call `ToListAsync()` early and filter the list in memory for flexibility
questions.explanation: Projecting with `Select`, using `AsNoTracking` for reads, and diagnosing with `ToQueryString()` are all on the checklist. Defaulting to lazy loading invites N+1, and materializing then filtering in memory is the anti-pattern the reviewer warns against.

questions.question: A single `SaveChanges` call that performs several inserts, updates, and deletes wraps them all in one transaction, so a partial failure rolls the entire batch back.
questions.explanation: `SaveChanges`/`SaveChangesAsync` is itself transactional: every change in one call is committed atomically and a partial failure rolls the whole batch back. You only need an explicit `BeginTransaction` to span *multiple* `SaveChanges` calls or external work.

questions.question: Explicit loading (`context.Entry(x).Reference(...).LoadAsync()`) inherently prevents the N+1 problem in all cases.
questions.explanation: Explicit loading only avoids N+1 when you load the whole related set in a single call. Calling `Load` once per item inside a loop reproduces N+1 by hand, just as lazy loading does.

questions.question: A team registers `DbContext` as a Singleton and a load test starts throwing "A second operation was started on this context before a previous operation completed." What is the root cause?
options: `DbContext` is not thread-safe; one shared instance is being used by concurrent requests, corrupting the change tracker
The connection pool is exhausted because the Singleton never releases its connection
`SaveChangesAsync` was used instead of `SaveChanges`, which serializes operations
Migrations were not applied, so EF retries the operation on a second thread
questions.explanation: A Singleton `DbContext` is shared across concurrent requests, but `DbContext` is not thread-safe — overlapping operations on one instance throw the "second operation" error. The fix is the Scoped (per-request) lifetime, or `AddDbContextFactory` where a non-scoped context is needed.

questions.question: Your model uses `[Timestamp] byte[] RowVersion` for optimistic concurrency. You migrate from SQL Server to PostgreSQL. What is the most accurate consequence?
options: `[Timestamp]`/`rowversion` is SQL Server-specific; on other providers you typically configure a manually-managed concurrency token (e.g. `IsConcurrencyToken()`) instead
Nothing changes — `[Timestamp]` is a provider-agnostic EF Core attribute the database auto-bumps everywhere
Optimistic concurrency stops working entirely; you must switch to pessimistic row locks
EF Core silently converts `RowVersion` to an auto-incrementing integer primary key
questions.explanation: `[Timestamp]`/`rowversion` relies on a SQL Server-specific auto-bumped column. Non-SQL-Server providers don't have it, so you use a manually-managed concurrency token via `IsConcurrencyToken()` (or `[ConcurrencyCheck]` on a property).

questions.question: After `context.Users.Add(user)` followed by a successful `SaveChangesAsync()`, what state is `user` in, and what would a subsequent property edit then produce on the next `SaveChanges`?
options: Unchanged after the save; editing a property flips it to Modified and the next `SaveChanges` emits an `UPDATE`
Detached after the save; an edit produces nothing because the entity is no longer tracked
Added still; a subsequent `SaveChanges` would `INSERT` the row a second time
Deleted after the save; an edit re-inserts the row on the next `SaveChanges`
questions.explanation: After a successful insert the entity transitions Added → Unchanged but stays tracked. Editing a tracked property flips Unchanged → Modified, so the next `SaveChanges` emits an `UPDATE` of the changed column.
